150.blurb: Duke's basketball program began in 1906 when athletic director Wilbur Wade Card introduced the sport to Trinity College, which later became Duke University. The 'Blue Devils' nickname was adopted in 1924, inspired by France's Chasseurs Alpins — elite Alpine soldiers known as 'les Diables Bleus' for their distinctive blue uniforms. Under Mike Krzyzewski, Duke won five national championships and became synonymous with college basketball excellence, producing six first-overall NBA draft picks. Cameron Indoor Stadium, home to the legendary 'Cameron Crazies,' has been one of the toughest environments in college sports since opening in 1940.
150.mascotOrigin: Inspired by France's Chasseurs Alpins, elite Alpine soldiers known as 'les Diables Bleus' during World War I, chosen by student newspaper editors in 1923
150.iconicMoment: Christian Laettner's turnaround buzzer-beater to defeat Kentucky 104-103 in overtime in the 1992 East Regional Final
150.funFact: Duke holds the best NCAA tournament winning percentage (.755) in history and has had six players selected first overall in the NBA Draft, the most of any school

12.blurb: Arizona's basketball program dates to the early 1900s, and the 'Wildcats' name was born in 1914 after a columnist wrote that the team 'showed the fight of wildcats.' The program became a national powerhouse under Lute Olson, who arrived in 1983 and built a dynasty in the desert. Arizona won its lone national championship in 1997, upsetting three No. 1 seeds en route to the title. McKale Center's court is named 'Lute and Bobbi Olson Court' in honor of the beloved coach and his late wife.
12.mascotOrigin: A 1914 Los Angeles Times columnist wrote Arizona 'showed the fight of wildcats' after a tough football game against Occidental College
12.iconicMoment: Winning the 1997 NCAA Championship, upsetting three No. 1 seeds including Kansas, North Carolina, and Kentucky
12.funFact: Arizona's original live mascot was a bobcat named Rufus Arizona, purchased for $9.41 in 1915, who sadly died a year later when he fell from a tree

130.blurb: Michigan's basketball program began in 1909 and won its sole NCAA Championship in 1989 when interim coach Steve Fisher guided the team to an overtime title win on Rumeal Robinson's clutch free throws. The program is best known for the 1991 'Fab Five' — Chris Webber, Jalen Rose, Juwan Howard, Ray Jackson, and Jimmy King — the first team to start five freshmen in an NCAA Championship game. Their baggy shorts and black socks revolutionized basketball fashion and culture. The Fab Five reached back-to-back title games in 1992 and 1993, though those appearances were later vacated.
130.mascotOrigin: The Wolverines name was adopted in the 1860s, tied to Michigan's historical association with the wolverine animal dating to territorial disputes with Ohio
130.iconicMoment: The Fab Five starting five freshmen in the 1992 NCAA Championship game, forever changing college basketball culture
130.funFact: The Fab Five's ESPN Films documentary became the highest-rated documentary in ESPN history, and their merchandise sales dwarfed even the 1989 championship team's

57.blurb: Florida's basketball program started in 1915 but spent decades in football's shadow before Billy Donovan transformed it into a national power after arriving in 1996. The 'Gators' nickname was chosen in 1911 as an homage to the alligators native to the Everglades region. Under Donovan, Florida became the first team to repeat as national champions with the same starting lineup, winning back-to-back titles in 2006 and 2007. The Gators added a third title in 2025, and remain the only school to have won both football and basketball national championships in the same year (2007).
57.mascotOrigin: Chosen in 1911 as a tribute to the alligators native to Florida's Everglades region
57.iconicMoment: Winning back-to-back NCAA Championships in 2006 and 2007 with the same starting five — the first team to accomplish this since Duke in 1991-92
57.funFact: Florida is the only school to win both football and basketball national championships in the same calendar year, accomplishing the feat in 2007

41.blurb: Connecticut played its first organized basketball game on February 8, 1901, a 17-12 win against Willimantic High School. The 'Huskies' name was adopted in 1934 via student poll, possibly a play on the Yukon/UConn homophone. UConn has won six NCAA championships — more than any school since the tournament expanded to 64 teams — and is the only university whose men's and women's basketball teams have both won national titles in the same year. Under Dan Hurley, UConn won back-to-back championships in 2023 and 2024, going undefeated in tournament play by double digits both years.
41.mascotOrigin: Adopted in 1934 via student poll after the school's name changed from Connecticut Agricultural College; the Yukon/UConn homophone may have influenced the choice
41.iconicMoment: Winning back-to-back NCAA Championships in 2023 and 2024 under Dan Hurley, with all 12 tournament wins coming by double digits
41.funFact: UConn is 6-0 in NCAA Championship games — a perfect record in title contests — and is the only school where both men's and women's basketball teams won national titles in the same year

127.blurb: Michigan State's basketball program dates to 1899, and the 'Spartans' name was chosen in honor of the ancient Greek warriors of Sparta renowned for their bravery. The program reached iconic status in 1979 when Earvin 'Magic' Johnson led the Spartans to the NCAA Championship in the most-watched college basketball game ever, defeating Larry Bird's Indiana State Sycamores. Under Tom Izzo, who arrived in 1995, Michigan State has reached ten Final Fours and won a second national title in 2000. ESPN named MSU the best basketball team of the 2000s decade.
127.mascotOrigin: Named after the ancient Greek warriors of Sparta, chosen for the bravery and discipline associated with the Spartan military tradition
127.iconicMoment: Magic Johnson's 24-point performance in the 1979 NCAA Championship game against Larry Bird's Indiana State — the most-watched college basketball game in history
127.funFact: Tom Izzo has led Michigan State to ten Final Fours, tied for sixth most all-time, and ESPN named the Spartans the best team of the 2000s decade

2509.blurb: Purdue played its first basketball game in 1896, a 34-19 win over the Lafayette YMCA, and has been a Big Ten powerhouse ever since. The 'Boilermakers' nickname originated in 1891 when a reporter described Purdue's 44-0 football rout of Wabash College. Purdue holds the most Big Ten regular season championships with 26 and was the first Big Ten program ranked No. 1 for three consecutive seasons. Despite producing legends like John Wooden and reaching multiple title games, the program is still chasing its first NCAA Championship.
2509.mascotOrigin: A Crawfordsville reporter dubbed the team 'Boiler Makers' after Purdue's 44-0 football trouncing of Wabash College on October 26, 1891
2509.iconicMoment: Zach Edey leading Purdue to the 2024 NCAA Championship game, the program's first Final Four in 44 years
2509.funFact: Purdue's official mascot is a full-size replica Victorian-era locomotive called the Boilermaker Special, claimed to be the world's largest, fastest, heaviest, and loudest collegiate mascot

2305.blurb: Kansas is the only college basketball program founded by the inventor of the game — Dr. James Naismith started the program in 1898, though he remains the school's only coach with a losing record. His protege Phog Allen, the 'Father of Basketball Coaching,' won 746 games and mentored future legends Adolph Rupp and Dean Smith. The Jayhawks have won four NCAA titles and are among the winningest programs in history. Allen Fieldhouse, named for Phog Allen with its court named for Naismith, has been called the loudest arena in college basketball.
2305.mascotOrigin: Named for 'Jayhawkers,' pro-Union guerrilla fighters from Kansas Territory during the Civil War era, with the bird mascot based on a combination of a blue jay and a hawk
2305.iconicMoment: Mario Chalmers' miraculous three-pointer to force overtime in the 2008 NCAA Championship game, which Kansas won over Memphis
2305.funFact: Kansas's coaching lineage is unmatched: Naismith coached Allen, who coached Dean Smith and Adolph Rupp — between them, they won over 3,000 games and multiple national titles

66.blurb: Iowa State's basketball program dates to 1907, and the Cyclones play in one of the most feared home courts in America — Hilton Coliseum. The school uses a cardinal named Cy as its mascot since you cannot dress up as a cyclone, though the athletic nickname references the powerful storms of the Great Plains. The term 'Hilton Magic' was coined in 1989 after Iowa State stunned third-ranked Missouri, and the atmosphere has been legendary ever since. Johnny Orr transformed the program in the 1980s, establishing the tradition of passionate basketball that continues under current coaches.
66.mascotOrigin: The 'Cyclones' name references the powerful storms of the Great Plains; the mascot Cy is a cardinal since the school colors (cardinal and gold) were adopted in 1899
66.iconicMoment: The birth of 'Hilton Magic' — coined by a Des Moines Register writer after Iowa State's upset of No. 3 Missouri on February 14, 1989
66.funFact: Hilton Coliseum was specifically built to hold in sound with solid concrete walls and steel doors, and opposing players have said the floor literally shakes from the crowd noise

2250.blurb: Gonzaga University, a small Jesuit school in Spokane, Washington founded in 1887, began playing basketball in 1907. Once known as the 'Fighting Irish' in the 1910s, the school switched to 'Bulldogs' in 1921. Gonzaga's magical 1999 NCAA Tournament run as a No. 10 seed — highlighted by CBS announcer Gus Johnson's famous 'The slipper still fits!' call — transformed the program from unknown to household name. Under Mark Few, who took over in 1999, Gonzaga has become one of the most consistent programs in the nation, including a 31-0 regular season in 2021.
2250.mascotOrigin: The school switched from 'Fighting Irish' to 'Bulldogs' in 1921; a live bulldog mascot named Salty once bit a referee during a 1967 game, making Sports Illustrated
2250.iconicMoment: The 1999 Cinderella run to the Elite Eight as a No. 10 seed, cementing Gonzaga as a national brand with CBS's Gus Johnson calling 'The slipper still fits!'
2250.funFact: When Gonzaga opened McCarthey Athletic Center in 2004, the Bulldogs won 100 consecutive home games there, the longest such streak in the NCAA at the time

96.blurb: Kentucky basketball was founded in 1903 and has grown into the sport's most passionate fan base, known as 'Big Blue Nation.' The 'Wildcats' nickname stuck after a 1909 football upset of Illinois. Under Adolph Rupp, the 'Baron of the Bluegrass,' Kentucky won four NCAA championships and 27 SEC titles. The program has won eight national championships overall, second only to UCLA. Rupp Arena, which opened in 1976 as the largest basketball-specific arena in the country, has a .892 all-time home winning percentage.
96.mascotOrigin: The 'Wildcats' nickname became associated with Kentucky after a 6-2 football victory over Illinois on October 9, 1909
96.iconicMoment: The 1996 NCAA Championship team, known as 'The Untouchables,' which brought Kentucky back to glory after years of NCAA sanctions
96.funFact: Kentucky has led the nation in home attendance since 1976 and won the national attendance title a record 25 times; Memorial Coliseum sold out all 345 home games during its 26-year tenure

153.blurb: North Carolina's basketball program began in 1911, and the 'Tar Heels' nickname traces to Civil War-era soldiers who proudly adopted the term rooted in the state's tar and pitch industry. Under Dean Smith, who coached for 36 years with a 96.6% graduation rate, UNC won two national championships and became a model of excellence. The program has won six NCAA titles overall, producing icons like Michael Jordan, whose game-winning shot in the 1982 championship game launched his legendary career. The Dean Smith Center, known as the 'Dean Dome,' stands as a monument to his legacy.
153.mascotOrigin: Traces to North Carolina's history as a leading tar and pitch producer; Civil War soldiers turned the once-derogatory 'tar heel' term into a badge of state pride
153.iconicMoment: Michael Jordan's game-winning jumper as a freshman to beat Georgetown 63-62 in the 1982 NCAA Championship game
153.funFact: When Dean Smith passed away in 2015, his will included a $200 check to every former player with a note reading 'Enjoy a dinner out compliments of Coach Dean Smith'

26.blurb: UCLA basketball began in 1919, and the school was originally known as the 'Cubs' before adopting 'Bruins' in 1926. John Wooden, the 'Wizard of Westwood,' built the greatest dynasty in college sports history, winning ten NCAA championships in twelve years including seven consecutive titles from 1967 to 1973. Before Wooden arrived in 1948, UCLA had never won a conference title and once lost 39 straight games to rival USC. The program produced legends like Kareem Abdul-Jabbar and Bill Walton and holds the record of 88 consecutive victories.
26.mascotOrigin: Originally called the 'Cubs' as the younger sibling of UC Berkeley, UCLA adopted 'Bruins' in 1926 when Berkeley gifted them the name
26.iconicMoment: The John Wooden dynasty — winning ten NCAA Championships in twelve years (1964-1975), including an unprecedented seven straight titles
26.funFact: UCLA's first live mascot was a stray dog named Rags found on campus by a gardener, and when Wooden arrived, he found lopsided basketballs and a gym smaller than his hometown high school's

222.blurb: Villanova's basketball program was established in 1920 at the private Catholic university outside Philadelphia. The Wildcats have won three national championships (1985, 2016, 2018) and are a cornerstone of Big East basketball. Their 1985 title as a No. 8 seed — shooting 78.6% from the field against Patrick Ewing's Georgetown in 'The Perfect Game' — remains the lowest seed ever to win the championship. Kris Jenkins' buzzer-beating three-pointer to beat North Carolina in the 2016 title game is considered one of the greatest shots in sports history.
222.mascotOrigin: The 'Wildcats' nickname has been used since the early days of the program, though the exact reason for its selection is lost to history
222.iconicMoment: Kris Jenkins' buzzer-beating three-pointer to give Villanova a 77-74 victory over North Carolina in the 2016 NCAA Championship game
222.funFact: In the 1985 championship game known as 'The Perfect Game,' Villanova shot 78.6% from the field — a championship game record that still stands — to upset heavily favored Georgetown

248.blurb: Houston's basketball program began in 1946 and rose to national prominence through Elvin Hayes and the famous 1968 'Game of the Century' — a nationally televised upset of top-ranked UCLA before 50,000 fans in the Astrodome. The Cougars reached their apex as 'Phi Slama Jama' from 1982-1984, featuring Hakeem Olajuwon and Clyde Drexler in an explosive, dunk-heavy attack. The nickname, coined by a Houston Post writer after a game with 29 dunks, captured an era. Houston won its first NCAA Championship in 2025, finally breaking through after heartbreaking losses in the 1980s.
248.mascotOrigin: Named by former head coach John Bender, who brought the 'Cougars' name from his previous job at Washington State because of his fondness for the animal
248.iconicMoment: The 'Phi Slama Jama' era (1982-84) featuring Hakeem Olajuwon and Clyde Drexler, with three consecutive Final Four appearances and a style that changed basketball forever
248.funFact: The Cougar Paw hand sign originated from an insult: in 1953, live mascot Shasta lost a toe in a cage door accident, and Texas students mocked UH by folding their thumbs — Houston fans later embraced the gesture as their own

84.blurb: Indiana's basketball program has competed since 1901 and holds a special place in the sport's history with five NCAA championships. The 1976 team, coached by Bob Knight, went 32-0 and remains the last team to finish a season undefeated and win the national title. Knight's fiery coaching style produced three championship teams and 661 wins at Indiana. The Hoosiers are known for their distinctive candy-striped warm-up pants and are one of the few programs that do not put players' names on the back of their jerseys.
84.mascotOrigin: The term 'Hoosier' has been associated with Indiana residents since the 1840s, though its exact origin remains debated among historians
84.iconicMoment: The 1976 undefeated season (32-0) under Bob Knight — the last team in men's Division I basketball to go wire-to-wire without a loss and win the national title
84.funFact: Indiana's distinctive candy-striped warm-up pants have been a tradition for decades, and the team is one of the few that refuses to put players' names on game jerseys

356.blurb: Illinois has been playing basketball since 1906 and ranks among the top 15 programs all-time in wins. The 'Fighting Illini' nickname first appeared in a 1911 newspaper article and references the Illiniwek confederation of Native American tribes. The 2005 team went 37-2, tying the record for most wins in a season, highlighted by a legendary 15-point comeback against Arizona in the Elite Eight. The 1989 'Flyin' Illini' under Lou Henson scored 100-plus points eight times, capturing the imagination of college basketball fans nationwide.
356.mascotOrigin: The 'Illini' name references the Illiniwek confederation; 'Fighting Illini' first appeared in a 1911 newspaper describing a basketball game against Purdue
356.iconicMoment: The 2005 Elite Eight comeback from 15 points down against Arizona with four minutes left, led by Luther Head and Deron Williams, to reach the Final Four
356.funFact: Despite being ranked No. 1 and undefeated, the 1943 'Whiz Kids' team opted not to play in the NCAA Tournament when three of its five starters were called to duty in World War II

333.blurb: Alabama's basketball program was founded in 1913 but spent most of its history in the shadow of the Crimson Tide's legendary football program. The 'Crimson Tide' nickname was coined by a sportswriter after a 1907 football game played in muddy conditions. Under Nate Oats, Alabama has emerged as a national basketball power with a fast-paced, three-point-heavy style, reaching their first Final Four in 2024. The undefeated 1929-30 team (20-0) was retroactively recognized as national champions.
333.mascotOrigin: Sports editor Hugh Roberts coined 'Crimson Tide' after watching Alabama play in the mud in 1907; the elephant mascot Big Al emerged from a 1930 sportswriter's description of players as 'Red Elephants'
333.iconicMoment: Reaching the program's first-ever Final Four in 2024, defeating No. 1 North Carolina and Clemson en route
333.funFact: Big Al the elephant mascot can make appearances at weddings for a $400 fee, with a maximum appearance time of one hour

258.blurb: Virginia's basketball program dates to 1905, and the 'Cavaliers' name references the Royalist supporters of King Charles I who are said to have fled to colonial Virginia. The program authored college basketball's greatest redemption story: one year after becoming the first No. 1 seed to lose to a No. 16 seed (falling to UMBC 74-54 in 2018), Virginia won the 2019 NCAA Championship in overtime against Texas Tech. In a remarkable twist, the coach who led UMBC to that historic upset, Ryan Odom, was later hired as Virginia's head coach in 2025.
258.mascotOrigin: Named for the Royalists of the English Civil War who remained loyal to King Charles I and were associated with colonial Virginia aristocracy
258.iconicMoment: Winning the 2019 NCAA Championship one year after the historic UMBC loss — NBC Sports called it 'the greatest redemption story in the history of sports'
258.funFact: The coach who led UMBC's historic 2018 upset of Virginia as a No. 16 seed, Ryan Odom, was hired as Virginia's head coach in 2025 after Tony Bennett's surprise retirement

2633.blurb: Tennessee's basketball program began in 1909, and the 'Volunteers' nickname comes from Tennessee's designation as 'The Volunteer State,' earned when overwhelming numbers of soldiers volunteered for the War of 1812 and the Mexican-American War. The mascot Smokey, a Bluetick Coonhound named for the Great Smoky Mountains, was selected in 1953 when a dog named Blue Smokey barked and howled at a halftime selection contest — prompting roaring student approval. Tennessee has been a consistent SEC contender with multiple Sweet Sixteen appearances and a tradition of passionate orange-clad fans.
2633.mascotOrigin: The 'Volunteers' nickname comes from Tennessee's role as 'The Volunteer State'; mascot Smokey is a Bluetick Coonhound named for the Great Smoky Mountains
2633.iconicMoment: The 2008 Sweet Sixteen run under Bruce Pearl, which reinvigorated Tennessee basketball and made Thompson-Boling Arena one of the toughest venues in the SEC
2633.funFact: Smokey II was dognapped by Kentucky students in 1955, and Smokey VIII had to have emergency veterinary care before the 1998 football championship game after eating a hotel washcloth

8.blurb: Arkansas fielded its first basketball team in 1924, a relatively late start, and the 'Razorbacks' name was born in 1909 when coach Hugo Bezdek said his team played 'like a wild band of razorback hogs.' The program reached its peak under Nolan Richardson, whose '40 Minutes of Hell' pressure defense delivered Arkansas its only NCAA Championship in 1994, defeating Duke 76-72. Richardson is the only coach in history to win a Division I title, an NIT title, and a junior college championship. The famous 'Hog Call' — Woo Pig Sooie — is the only cheer of its kind in college sports.
8.mascotOrigin: Coach Hugo Bezdek said his team played 'like a wild band of razorback hogs' after a 16-0 win over LSU in 1909; students voted to change the mascot from Cardinals to Razorbacks in 1910
8.iconicMoment: Winning the 1994 NCAA Championship under Nolan Richardson's '40 Minutes of Hell' — Scotty Thurman's key three-pointer sealed the 76-72 win over Duke
8.funFact: Nolan Richardson is the only coach in basketball history to win the NCAA Championship, the NIT Championship, and a Junior College National Championship

97.blurb: Louisville's basketball program was founded in 1911 and became a national force under Denny Crum, who coached for 30 seasons and earned the nickname 'Cool Hand Luke' for his sideline composure. The Cardinals won NCAA titles in 1980, led by Darrell Griffith's 'Doctors of Dunk,' and in 1986, when freshman Pervis Ellison — dubbed 'Never Nervous' — led them past Duke. Louisville is the only school in the nation to have claimed championships in three major postseason tournaments: the 1948 NAIB, the 1956 NIT, and the NCAA Tournament.
97.mascotOrigin: The Cardinal Bird has been Louisville's mascot, representing the state bird of Kentucky
97.iconicMoment: The 1986 NCAA Championship, where freshman 'Never Nervous' Pervis Ellison led Louisville to the title over Duke in Denny Crum's second championship
97.funFact: Louisville is the only school to have won the NAIB tournament (1948), the NIT (1956), and the NCAA Championship (1980, 1986) — claiming a triple crown of postseason basketball

194.blurb: Ohio State's basketball program formed in 1898 and appeared in the very first NCAA Championship game in 1939, losing to Oregon. The 'Buckeye' name comes from the Ohio state tree; the mascot Brutus Buckeye — a student wearing a giant buckeye nut head — debuted in 1965. The Buckeyes won their lone national championship in 1960, led by future Hall of Famers Jerry Lucas and John Havlicek. A little-known fact: future Indiana coaching legend Bob Knight was a reserve on that championship team.
194.mascotOrigin: Named after the Ohio buckeye tree, the state tree of Ohio; 'Buckeye' has referred to Ohio residents since 1788 and was formally adopted as the school nickname in 1950
194.iconicMoment: The 1960 NCAA Championship, led by Jerry Lucas and John Havlicek, defeating California 75-55 for Ohio State's only basketball title
194.funFact: Future Indiana coaching legend Bob Knight was a reserve player on Ohio State's 1960 championship team, sitting behind Hall of Famers Jerry Lucas and John Havlicek

2599.blurb: St. John's basketball was founded in 1907 and the 1910-11 team went undefeated at 14-0, earning a retroactive national championship. Originally known as the 'Redmen' for their all-red uniforms, the school changed to 'Red Storm' in 1994. Under Lou Carnesecca, St. John's became a Big East powerhouse in the 1980s, reaching the Final Four in 1985 behind All-American Chris Mullin. The program has played at Madison Square Garden every year since 1931 — the longest continuous relationship between a team and a venue in college basketball.
2599.mascotOrigin: Originally called 'Redmen' after their all-red athletic uniforms in the 1920s; changed to 'Red Storm' in 1994 to move away from perceived Native American references
2599.iconicMoment: The 1985 Big East Championship 'Sweater Game' between Lou Carnesecca and Georgetown's John Thompson, featuring Chris Mullin vs. Patrick Ewing
2599.funFact: St. John's has played at least one game at Madison Square Garden every year since 1931, the longest continuous relationship between a team and venue in college basketball history

275.blurb: Wisconsin's basketball program dates to the 1890s, making it one of the oldest in the Big Ten. The 'Badgers' nickname refers to 1820s lead miners who lived in hillside tunnels 'like badgers' during Wisconsin winters. The Badgers won their only NCAA Championship in 1941 in their first-ever tournament appearance. The program experienced a modern renaissance with 19 consecutive NCAA Tournament appearances from 1999-2017 and back-to-back Final Four runs in 2014 and 2015. Bucky Badger's full name is 'Buckingham U. Badger,' from the fight song urging the team to 'buck right through that line.'
275.mascotOrigin: Named for 1820s lead miners who lived in hillside tunnels 'like badgers' during harsh Wisconsin winters; Wisconsin was dubbed the 'Badger State' as a result
275.iconicMoment: Back-to-back Final Four appearances in 2014 and 2015, including a run to the 2015 National Championship game
275.funFact: Wisconsin tried to replace Bucky Badger with a cow named 'Henrietta Holstein' in the 1970s, but the university ultimately kept Bucky — whose full name is 'Buckingham U. Badger'

252.blurb: BYU's basketball program was established in 1902, making it one of the oldest west of the Mississippi. The 'Cougars' name was adopted in 1924 when athletic director George Cobabe chose the animal for its strength and courage. BYU captured national attention in 2011 when Jimmer Fredette's sensational scoring sparked 'Jimmermania' — earning him the Naismith Award while leading the country in scoring. The program has won 27 conference championships and produced NBA talent like Danny Ainge. BYU joined the Big 12 Conference in 2023.
252.mascotOrigin: Athletic director George Cobabe chose 'Cougars' in 1924 for the animal's qualities of strength and courage
252.iconicMoment: Jimmer Fredette's 2011 'Jimmermania' season — scoring 52 points against New Mexico and winning the Naismith Award as national player of the year
252.funFact: Jimmer Fredette dropped 49 points at Arizona's McKale Center in 2009, still the most points ever scored by a visiting player there — the Arizona fans gave him a standing ovation

152.blurb: NC State began basketball in 1911 and adopted the 'Wolfpack' nickname in 1921. The program has won two NCAA Championships, but none is more famous than 1983 under Jim Valvano. As a No. 6 seed, the 'Cardiac Pack' pulled off improbable last-second wins throughout the tournament, culminating in Lorenzo Charles' buzzer-beating dunk to stun Houston's 'Phi Slama Jama' 54-52 for the title. Valvano's famous image, running across the court looking for someone to hug, remains one of basketball's most iconic scenes.
152.mascotOrigin: The 'Wolfpack' nickname was adopted in 1921; NC State was one of the first universities to federally trademark its mascot and logos in 1983
152.iconicMoment: Lorenzo Charles' buzzer-beating dunk off Dereck Whittenburg's airball to beat Houston's Phi Slama Jama 54-52 in the 1983 NCAA Championship
152.funFact: Jim Valvano's famous ESPY speech included the line 'Don't give up, don't ever give up' — the Jimmy V Foundation he inspired has raised over $150 million for cancer research

158.blurb: Nebraska's basketball program was founded in 1897, making it one of the oldest in the country, though football has historically dominated the school's identity. Before 1900, teams went by names like 'Bugeaters' and 'Rattlesnake Boys' until Lincoln sportswriter Charles Sherman borrowed the 'Cornhuskers' name from Iowa, whose fans preferred 'Hawkeyes.' The nickname became so popular it eventually became the official name of the entire state. Under Fred Hoiberg and then Matt Abdelmassih, Nebraska has worked to elevate its basketball profile, earning a No. 4 seed in the 2026 NCAA Tournament.
158.mascotOrigin: Sportswriter Charles Sherman coined 'Cornhuskers' around 1900, borrowing it from Iowa (whose fans preferred 'Hawkeyes'); the name stuck and became the state's official nickname too
158.iconicMoment: Danny Nee's 1990s era brought four consecutive NCAA Tournament appearances and made the Devaney Center a feared venue
158.funFact: Nebraska's inflatable mascot Lil' Red, introduced in 1993, was the first inflatable mascot in either college or professional sports history, standing 8 feet 4 inches tall

2628.blurb: TCU has played basketball since 1909, and the 'Horned Frogs' nickname — which actually refers to the Texas horned lizard — predates the university's current name, having been adopted in 1897. The school won ten Southwest Conference titles in its history and has been a member of the Big 12 since 2012. In 2022, TCU won its first NCAA Tournament game since 1987, signaling a resurgence for the program. SuperFrog, the costumed mascot since 1979, was named one of Sports Illustrated's Top 25 College Mascots.
2628.mascotOrigin: Named for the Texas horned lizard (commonly called a 'horned frog') in 1897, a year before the school was even named TCU; legend says the campus was covered with the creatures
2628.iconicMoment: The 2022 NCAA Tournament victory over Seton Hall, the program's first tournament win in 35 years, signaling TCU basketball's return to relevance
2628.funFact: The Texas horned lizard can shoot blood up to five feet from its eyes as a defense mechanism — fitting for one of college sports' most unique mascots

228.blurb: Clemson's basketball program was first sponsored in 1912 and won its first four games that season. The Tigers are a founding member of the ACC and have built a competitive program highlighted by multiple NCAA Tournament appearances. Clemson's iconic Tiger Paw logo, introduced in 1970, is one of the most recognizable symbols in college sports. The program reached the Elite Eight in both 1980 — when they upset No. 1 Duke in overtime — and again in 2024, marking the high points of Clemson basketball history.
228.mascotOrigin: The 'Tigers' name reflects the school's orange and purple colors; Clemson's iconic Tiger Paw logo was introduced on this date in 1970 and has become one of college sports' most recognizable symbols
228.iconicMoment: Upsetting No. 1 ranked Duke 87-82 in overtime at Littlejohn Coliseum on January 9, 1980, before the largest crowd in arena history
228.funFact: Clemson's first indoor basketball game on campus in 1913 was played in the basement of the administration building, with players reportedly dribbling around the support pillars

183.blurb: Syracuse's basketball program began around 1900 and ranks fifth all-time in total wins among NCAA programs. Jim Boeheim, a former walk-on who earned a scholarship, coached the Orange for 47 years from 1976 to 2023 — the longest tenure at one school in Division I history. His signature 2-3 zone defense led Syracuse to the 2003 national championship behind freshman Carmelo Anthony. The JMA Wireless Dome (formerly Carrier Dome) is the largest arena in college basketball with a capacity exceeding 35,000, and Syracuse has led the nation in attendance 25 times.
183.mascotOrigin: The teams were known as the 'Orangemen' for most of their history before shortening to 'Orange' in 2004, reflecting the school's primary color
183.iconicMoment: Carmelo Anthony leading Syracuse to the 2003 NCAA Championship as a freshman, scoring 20 points in the title game victory over Kansas
183.funFact: Jim Boeheim was a skinny walk-on in 1963 who shared a backcourt with Hall of Famer Dave Bing — he went on to spend his entire adult life at Syracuse as player, assistant, and head coach

46.blurb: Georgetown played its first basketball game on February 9, 1907, defeating Virginia 22-11. The 'Hoya' name derives from the college cheer 'Hoya Saxa,' a mix of Greek and Latin roughly meaning 'what rocks' — a nod to the school's stone walls. John Thompson made history in 1984 as the first African-American coach to win an NCAA Championship, led by the dominant Patrick Ewing. Georgetown's basketball alumni include four Naismith Hall of Famers: Ewing, Alonzo Mourning, Dikembe Mutombo, and Allen Iverson.
46.mascotOrigin: From the cheer 'Hoya Saxa' — Greek 'hoia' (what) and Latin 'saxa' (rocks), meaning 'what rocks,' referencing the school's stone walls; the bulldog mascot is named Jack
46.iconicMoment: John Thompson becoming the first African-American coach to win the NCAA Championship in 1984, led by Patrick Ewing's dominant performance
46.funFact: Georgetown's bulldog mascot was supposed to be named 'Hoya,' but the dog only responded to 'Jack' — so every Georgetown bulldog mascot since has been named Jack

269.blurb: Marquette basketball dates to 1916 and the program won its lone NCAA Championship in 1977 — Al McGuire's final game as coach, having announced his mid-season retirement. Previously known as the 'Warriors,' Marquette changed to 'Golden Eagles' in 1994 out of respect for Native American communities. Dwyane Wade put Marquette back on the national stage in 2003, recording a triple-double in the Elite Eight upset of Kentucky to reach the Final Four. The program now plays at Fiserv Forum, sharing an arena with the NBA's Milwaukee Bucks.
269.mascotOrigin: Changed from 'Warriors' to 'Golden Eagles' in 1994 out of respect for Native American communities; the mascot is named 'Iggy' after St. Ignatius of Loyola
269.iconicMoment: Al McGuire winning the 1977 NCAA Championship in his final game as coach, having announced his retirement mid-season — he broke down in tears as time expired
269.funFact: Dwyane Wade was only recruited by Marquette, Illinois State, and DePaul due to low ACT scores, and he had to sit out his freshman year — he went on to become the highest-drafted player in Marquette history

2641.blurb: Texas Tech's basketball program began in 1925, and the 'Red Raiders' name was coined by a sports editor in 1936, inspired by the team's all-red uniforms and demanding schedule. The school has two mascots: the Masked Rider, a horseback tradition since 1936, and Raider Red, a costumed cowboy created in 1971 when opposing schools banned live horses. Texas Tech reached the 2019 NCAA Championship game in a thrilling overtime loss to Virginia, marking the program's greatest run. The 'Guns Up' hand sign, introduced in the 1970s, has become one of college sports' most recognizable gestures.
2641.mascotOrigin: Sports editor Collier Parrish coined 'Red Raiders' in 1936 for the team's all-red uniforms; the Masked Rider tradition began that same year with 'ghost riders' circling the field
2641.iconicMoment: Reaching the 2019 NCAA Championship game under Chris Beard, the program's first-ever title game appearance, falling to Virginia in overtime
2641.funFact: The first Raider Red mascot head was built from chicken wire and papier-mache and was so lightweight it could have doubled as a pinata — a new head was built every season until the 1990s

238.blurb: Vanderbilt claims one of basketball's earliest origins, defeating the Nashville YMCA 9-6 on February 7, 1893 — just two years after James Naismith invented the sport. The 'Commodores' nickname honors founder Cornelius Vanderbilt, a steamship entrepreneur whose rank of Commodore was the highest in the U.S. Navy. As the SEC's only private university, Vanderbilt has carved out a unique niche, with Memorial Gymnasium's distinctive end-of-the-floor bench setup creating one of basketball's most unusual home-court advantages. Perry Wallace broke the SEC's color barrier when he became the conference's first Black varsity basketball player in 1967.
238.mascotOrigin: Named for university founder Cornelius Vanderbilt, a steamship tycoon known as 'the Commodore' — the highest rank in the U.S. Navy at the time
238.iconicMoment: Perry Wallace breaking the SEC's color barrier as the conference's first African-American varsity basketball player from 1967-1970, facing hostility at opposing venues
238.funFact: Vanderbilt won the 1951 SEC Tournament in an upset over Kentucky, and legend has it that 'Kentucky' was already inscribed on the championship trophy before the Commodores' victory

2608.blurb: Saint Mary's College, a small Catholic school founded in 1863 in Moraga, California, has been playing basketball since the early 1900s. The 'Gaels' name was bestowed in 1926 by a San Francisco sportswriter, referencing the Irish warrior tradition fitting for a school founded by the Irish Catholic Brothers. Under Randy Bennett, who inherited a 2-27 team in 2001, the Gaels have become one of America's premier mid-major programs and a perennial WCC contender. Saint Mary's has a tradition of recruiting Australian players, having at least one on the roster every season since Bennett's arrival.
2608.mascotOrigin: Sportswriter Pat Frayne christened the teams 'The Gaels' in 1926, referencing Irish warriors — fitting for a school founded by Irish Catholic Christian Brothers
2608.iconicMoment: The 2010 NCAA Tournament run, where Omar Samhan led Saint Mary's past No. 2 seed Villanova to reach the Sweet Sixteen for the first time
2608.funFact: Two-time Oscar-winning actor Mahershala Ali played basketball at Saint Mary's under his original name, Mahershalalhashbaz Gilmore

2294.blurb: Iowa's basketball program became varsity in 1901 and joined the Big Ten in 1908. The 'Hawkeye' name comes from James Fenimore Cooper's novel 'The Last of the Mohicans' and was adopted as Iowa's state nickname in 1838. The Hawkeyes reached the NCAA Championship game in 1956, falling to Bill Russell's undefeated San Francisco team. Carver-Hawkeye Arena, known as 'The House That Lute Built' after coach Lute Olson championed its construction, opened in 1983 with innovative geothermal heating and most seating below ground level.
2294.mascotOrigin: The 'Hawkeye' name comes from the hero in James Fenimore Cooper's 'The Last of the Mohicans' and was adopted as Iowa's state nickname in 1838
2294.iconicMoment: Reaching the 1956 NCAA Championship game against Bill Russell's unbeaten San Francisco Dons — Iowa's only title game appearance
2294.funFact: Carver-Hawkeye Arena is called 'The House That Lute Built' after coach Lute Olson championed its construction; the mascot Herky the Hawk even served as military insignia for the 124th Fighting Squadron during the Korean War

2670.blurb: VCU's basketball program was founded in 1968 after the merger of the Richmond Professional Institute and the Medical College of Virginia. The 'Rams' name was adopted in 1970, influenced by Richmond's colonial-era sheep farming history. VCU pulled off one of the most incredible tournament runs ever in 2011, becoming the first team to advance from the First Four to the Final Four under Shaka Smart's 'Havoc' defense. The Rams upset Georgetown, Purdue, Florida State, and top-seeded Kansas in consecutive games.
2670.mascotOrigin: Originally named the 'Panthers' in 1968, VCU changed to 'Rams' in 1970, influenced by Richmond's colonial-era sheep farming history
2670.iconicMoment: The 2011 First Four-to-Final Four run under Shaka Smart, the first team ever to accomplish this feat, defeating five opponents including top-seeded Kansas
2670.funFact: VCU's 2011 Final Four run was the first time a team advanced from the First Four play-in games all the way to the Final Four — coach Shaka Smart was named after the Zulu monarch Shaka kaSenzangakhona

2116.blurb: UCF's basketball program began in the late 1960s before the school even had a nickname — the original proposed mascot was the 'Citronaut,' a bizarre orange-astronaut hybrid honoring Central Florida's citrus and space industries. Students eventually chose 'Knights' in 1970, allied with the university seal's Pegasus symbol. The program gained national attention when 7-foot-6 Tacko Fall, a Senegalese player who maintained a 4.0 high school GPA and scored in the 95th percentile on the SAT, became one of college basketball's most beloved figures from 2016-2019.
2116.mascotOrigin: The original mascot was the 'Citronaut' — an orange with an astronaut helmet; students voted for 'Knights' in 1970, connected to the Pegasus on the university seal
2116.iconicMoment: Tacko Fall's 2019 NCAA Tournament appearance, bringing national attention to UCF basketball as the 7-foot-6 center nearly led an upset of Duke
2116.funFact: UCF's original mascot proposal was the 'Citronaut' — a half-orange, half-astronaut creature honoring the area's citrus and space industries — which was rejected by students but has become a beloved retro symbol

2390.blurb: Miami's basketball program launched in 1926, just a year after the university's founding. The 'Hurricanes' name was chosen after a devastating 1926 hurricane postponed the opening football game — players suggested the name hoping they would 'sweep away opponents' like the storm. The program was actually shut down from 1971-1985 due to poor facilities and attendance, but was revived and has since become a competitive ACC member. Sebastian the Ibis serves as mascot — chosen because the ibis is the last bird to flee an approaching hurricane and the first to reappear.
2390.mascotOrigin: Players chose 'Hurricanes' in 1926 after a real hurricane postponed their opening game; the ibis mascot was chosen because it is the last bird to flee and first to return after a hurricane
2390.iconicMoment: Winning the 2013 ACC regular season and tournament championships under Jim Larranaga, marking Miami's emergence as a legitimate ACC basketball power
2390.funFact: In 1989, mascot Sebastian the Ibis was tackled and handcuffed by police after trying to extinguish Florida State's flaming spear with a fire extinguisher while wearing a fireman's helmet

142.blurb: Missouri's basketball program was founded in 1907, and the 'Tigers' nickname dates to the Civil War when a local militia called 'The Missouri Tigers' defended Columbia from guerrilla raiders — who never attacked, as the Tigers' fierce reputation preceded them. Norm Stewart, a Missouri native who played both basketball and baseball for the school, coached the Tigers for 32 seasons and won more games (634) than were won in the 60 years of the program before him. Stewart also founded the Coaches vs. Cancer initiative, which has raised over $150 million.
142.mascotOrigin: Named after a Civil War-era militia that defended Columbia, Missouri from guerrilla raiders; mascot Truman is named after President Harry Truman, the only Missouri-born president
142.iconicMoment: The 1994 team going a perfect 14-0 in Big Eight conference play under Norm Stewart, who was named AP Coach of the Year
142.funFact: Norm Stewart refused to let his team stop for gas, food, or lodging in the state of Kansas during road trips, not wanting any Missouri tax dollars going to the rival Jayhawks' state

251.blurb: Texas basketball dates to 1906 at the state's flagship university, which opened in 1883. The live mascot Bevo, a Longhorn steer, has been a tradition since 1916 — the first Bevo was famously barbecued at a football banquet and reportedly the meat was tough. While Texas has historically been a football school, the basketball program produced Kevin Durant, who played just one season in 2007 before becoming one of the NBA's greatest scorers. The Longhorns have been regular NCAA Tournament participants and joined the SEC in 2024.
251.mascotOrigin: Named for the Texas Longhorn cattle; the first live mascot Bevo arrived in 1916 after an alumnus bought an orange-tinted steer with $1 donations from 124 alumni
251.iconicMoment: Kevin Durant's dominant 2007 freshman season, averaging 25.8 points per game and winning the Naismith Award before being selected second overall in the NBA Draft
251.funFact: The first Bevo was famously barbecued and served at a football banquet — according to The Alcalde, the meat was tough; Texas is currently on Bevo XV

245.blurb: Texas A&M, the state's first public university (opened 1876), has fielded basketball teams since around 1912. The 'Aggies' nickname reflects the school's agricultural roots, formally adopted in the 1920s. Reveille, a dog the school's first live mascot since 1931, is the highest-ranking member of the Corps of Cadets and tradition holds that if she barks in class, the professor should dismiss the students. The basketball program experienced a surge under Billy Gillispie in 2007, spending most of the season in the top 10 and becoming the first Big 12 South team to win at Kansas.
245.mascotOrigin: The 'Aggies' name reflects the school's agricultural origins; mascot Reveille — a stray dog hit by cadets' car in 1931 — got her name when she barked at the morning bugle call
245.iconicMoment: The 2007 season under Billy Gillispie, when A&M spent most of the year in the top 10 and became the first Big 12 South team to win at Allen Fieldhouse
245.funFact: Reveille holds the rank of five-diamond general in the Corps of Cadets, outranking every student including the Corps commander, and tradition says if she falls asleep on a cadet's bed, the cadet must sleep elsewhere

61.blurb: Georgia's basketball program dates to the early 1900s, and the 'Bulldogs' nickname was adopted in 1920 due to the school's historical ties to Yale, whose mascot is also a Bulldog — Georgia's first president was a Yale graduate. The live English Bulldog mascot Uga has been provided by the Seiler family since 1956, when a law student brought his wedding-gift puppy to a football game. Anthony Edwards became the first Georgia basketball player selected first overall in the NBA Draft in 2020. Georgia is the only major college that buries its mascots inside the stadium.
61.mascotOrigin: The 'Bulldogs' nickname was adopted in 1920, partly due to ties to Yale (whose first president was a Yale grad); the live Uga mascot has been owned by the Seiler family since 1956
61.iconicMoment: Reaching the 1983 Final Four, the deepest NCAA Tournament run in program history
61.funFact: Georgia is the only major college that buries its mascots inside the stadium — every Uga has a bronze epitaph at Sanford Stadium, and flowers are placed on their graves before each home game

156.blurb: Creighton, a Jesuit university in Omaha founded in 1878, began basketball in 1907 and adopted the 'Bluejays' name in 1923 via a public contest coordinated by the Omaha Bee newspaper. Doug McDermott, nicknamed 'Dougie McBuckets,' left Creighton as the fifth-leading scorer in NCAA history with 3,150 points — all while playing for his father, coach Greg McDermott. He even relinquished his scholarship as a senior to make room for a teammate, becoming perhaps the greatest walk-on in Big East history. Baseball Hall of Famer Bob Gibson also played basketball at Creighton.
156.mascotOrigin: The 'Bluejays' name was selected from 200 entries in a 1923 public contest because the bird's color matched the school's blue and white colors
156.iconicMoment: Doug McDermott's 2014 Senior Night, scoring a career-high 45 points against Providence and surpassing 3,000 career points
156.funFact: Baseball Hall of Famer Bob Gibson played basketball at Creighton before his legendary pitching career, finishing as the school's third-leading scorer with 1,272 points in 1957

139.blurb: Saint Louis University's basketball program dates to around 1900, making it one of the oldest in the Midwest. The 'Billikens' name — one of college sports' most unusual nicknames — was adopted in 1911 after a law student sculpted a figurine of coach John Bender that resembled a popular 'Billiken' good-luck charm of the era. Saint Louis won the NIT Championship in 1948, then college basketball's most prestigious tournament, under coach Eddie Hickey. As a founding member of the Missouri Valley Conference, SLU has a proud tradition in the Atlantic 10.
139.mascotOrigin: A law student's figurine of coach John Bender resembled a popular 'Billiken' good-luck charm from the era, and the name was adopted in 1911
139.iconicMoment: Winning the 1948 NIT Championship under coach Eddie Hickey, at a time when the NIT was considered college basketball's premier postseason tournament
139.funFact: The Billiken is actually a patented charm doll created by a Kansas City art teacher in 1908 — making Saint Louis one of the few schools whose mascot originated as a mass-produced good-luck figurine

2541.blurb: Santa Clara University, a Jesuit institution founded in 1851, has played basketball since the early 1900s. The 'Broncos' nickname reflects the school's California ranching heritage. Santa Clara's greatest moment came in 1952 when the Broncos upset heavily favored UCLA to advance to their one and only Final Four appearance. The program has produced NBA standout Steve Nash, a two-time NBA MVP who played for the Broncos from 1992-1996, putting the small West Coast Conference school on the national map.
2541.mascotOrigin: The 'Broncos' nickname reflects Santa Clara's California ranching heritage and the untamed spirit of the wild horse
2541.iconicMoment: Steve Nash's college career from 1992-1996, turning a small Jesuit school into a nationally recognized basketball program
2541.funFact: Two-time NBA MVP Steve Nash chose Santa Clara over other schools and led the Broncos to three consecutive NCAA Tournament appearances in the mid-1990s

328.blurb: Utah State's basketball program dates to 1902 at the land-grant university in Logan, Utah. Like many agricultural schools, the 'Aggies' name reflects the university's farming roots. The Dee Glen Smith Spectrum, known simply as 'The Spectrum,' is considered one of the most hostile environments in college basketball thanks to the famed student section 'The HURD.' Utah State has been a mid-major powerhouse, particularly under Stew Morrill, who won 402 games and led the Aggies to multiple NCAA Tournament appearances during his tenure from 1998-2015.
328.mascotOrigin: The 'Aggies' name reflects Utah State's founding as an agricultural college, common among land-grant universities
328.iconicMoment: The 2011 NCAA Tournament upset of fifth-seeded Kansas State, showcasing the Aggies' ability to compete with power-conference programs
328.funFact: The Spectrum's student section, known as 'The HURD' (an acronym for 'Hostile, Utah-bred, Rowdy, and Dedicated'), is regularly cited as one of the most intense student sections in the country

2132.blurb: Cincinnati's basketball program dates to 1901 and produced one of the sport's greatest dynasties in the early 1960s. The 'Bearcats' name was adopted in 1914 when a cheerleader combined 'bear' and 'cats' after a cartoon of a ferocious bear-cat hybrid. Under Ed Jucker, Cincinnati won back-to-back NCAA Championships in 1961 and 1962, becoming the last team to win consecutive titles until Duke in 1991-92. The program also featured Oscar Robertson, widely considered one of the greatest players in basketball history, who averaged 33.8 points per game during his college career.
2132.mascotOrigin: Adopted in 1914 when cheerleader Norman 'Pat' Lyon combined 'bear' and 'cats' after a cartoon of a ferocious bear-cat hybrid appeared in the school newspaper
2132.iconicMoment: Winning back-to-back NCAA Championships in 1961 and 1962 under coach Ed Jucker, the last team to repeat until Duke thirty years later
2132.funFact: Oscar Robertson, 'The Big O,' averaged 33.8 points per game during his three-year varsity career at Cincinnati (1957-1960) and is widely considered one of the five greatest basketball players ever

58.blurb: USF's basketball program launched in 1959, just three years after the university opened in 1956. The 'Bulls' name was chosen to represent strength and the cattle ranching heritage of Central Florida. The program's history includes stretches of strong play in the American Athletic Conference and Conference USA, with several postseason appearances. Under current coach Amir Abdur-Rahim, USF earned its first NCAA Tournament at-large bid in decades for the 2024 tournament, and was selected as an 11-seed in the 2026 tournament, marking a new era for Bulls basketball.
58.mascotOrigin: The 'Bulls' nickname was adopted to represent strength and Florida's cattle ranching heritage; the mascot Rocky the Bull has been a fixture since the school's early years
58.iconicMoment: Earning a 2024 NCAA Tournament bid under Amir Abdur-Rahim, the program's first at-large selection in the modern era
58.funFact: USF is one of the youngest major basketball programs in the country, having been founded just three years after the university itself opened in 1956

47.blurb: Howard University, a historically Black university founded in 1867 in Washington, D.C., has fielded basketball teams since the 1920s. The 'Bison' mascot reflects strength and resilience, fitting for a school named after Civil War general Oliver O. Howard. Howard competes in the MEAC and earned a spot in the 2026 NCAA Tournament, representing HBCU basketball on the national stage. The university has produced more Black PhD recipients than any institution in America, extending its legacy of excellence beyond athletics.
47.mascotOrigin: The 'Bison' mascot was chosen to represent strength, resilience, and the American spirit, fitting for a school named after Civil War general Oliver O. Howard
47.iconicMoment: Earning a spot in the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a 16-seed, representing HBCU basketball on the national stage
47.funFact: Howard University has produced more Black PhD recipients than any university in America, and its athletic programs have been a cornerstone of HBCU sports tradition

2567.blurb: SMU's basketball program began in 1917 at the Dallas-based university founded in 1911. The 'Mustangs' name was adopted in 1917, inspired by the wild horses of the American Southwest. SMU was a founding member of the Southwest Conference and won multiple SWC titles before the conference dissolved. The program has experienced a resurgence in recent years, joining the ACC in 2024 after successful runs in the American Athletic Conference. Moody Coliseum has been the Mustangs' home court since 1956.
2567.mascotOrigin: The 'Mustangs' name was adopted in 1917, inspired by the wild horses native to the American Southwest and Texas plains
2567.iconicMoment: The 2015 season when SMU went 27-7 and earned a No. 6 seed in the NCAA Tournament under Larry Brown, marking the program's return to national relevance
2567.funFact: SMU's most famous basketball alum is Jim Krebs, who played on the Lakers alongside Jerry West and Elgin Baylor in the early 1960s

152.blurb: NC State began basketball in 1911 and adopted the 'Wolfpack' nickname in 1921. The program has won two NCAA Championships, but none is more famous than 1983 under Jim Valvano. As a No. 6 seed, the 'Cardiac Pack' pulled off improbable last-second wins throughout the tournament, culminating in Lorenzo Charles' buzzer-beating dunk to stun Houston's 'Phi Slama Jama' 54-52 for the title. Valvano's famous image, running across the court looking for someone to hug, remains one of basketball's most iconic scenes.
152.mascotOrigin: The 'Wolfpack' nickname was adopted in 1921; NC State was one of the first universities to federally trademark its mascot and logos in 1983
152.iconicMoment: Lorenzo Charles' buzzer-beating dunk off Dereck Whittenburg's airball to beat Houston's Phi Slama Jama 54-52 in the 1983 NCAA Championship
152.funFact: Jim Valvano's famous ESPY speech included the line 'Don't give up, don't ever give up' — the Jimmy V Foundation he inspired has raised over $150 million for cancer research

277.blurb: West Virginia's basketball program dates to 1903 and the 'Mountaineers' name reflects the rugged Appalachian terrain of the state. WVU's greatest basketball achievement was reaching the 2010 Final Four under Bob Huggins, a proud West Virginia native who returned home after coaching at Cincinnati. Jerry West, widely considered one of the greatest basketball players ever, played at WVU from 1957-1960 and led the Mountaineers to the 1959 NCAA Championship game. His silhouette became the inspiration for the NBA logo.
277.mascotOrigin: The 'Mountaineers' name reflects West Virginia's Appalachian mountain terrain and the rugged spirit of the state's residents
277.iconicMoment: Jerry West leading West Virginia to the 1959 NCAA Championship game, averaging 32.2 points per game during the tournament run
277.funFact: Jerry West's silhouette became the inspiration for the NBA logo, and he remains the only player to win the NBA Finals MVP from the losing team (1969 Lakers)

2579.blurb: South Carolina's basketball program dates to 1908, and the 'Gamecocks' nickname — one of the most unique in college sports — was adopted in honor of Revolutionary War hero Thomas Sumter, known as the 'Fighting Gamecock' for his aggressive tactics. The program reached its peak under Frank McGuire, who led the Gamecocks to their only Final Four appearance in 1970. South Carolina joined the SEC in 1991 and has been building its basketball tradition in one of football's most dominant conferences.
2579.mascotOrigin: Named in honor of Revolutionary War general Thomas Sumter, called the 'Fighting Gamecock' by the British for his fierce combat style
2579.iconicMoment: The 2017 NCAA Tournament run to the Final Four, the program's first since 1970 and only the second in school history
2579.funFact: South Carolina is one of only two SEC schools with a Gamecock mascot — the live rooster mascot Sir Big Spur attends football games wearing garnet accessories

2086.blurb: Butler University, founded in 1855 in Indianapolis, has played basketball since 1893. The 'Bulldogs' name was adopted in the early 1900s, fitting the school's tenacious spirit. Butler became college basketball's ultimate underdog story with back-to-back NCAA Championship game appearances in 2010 and 2011 under Brad Stevens, who later became head coach of the Boston Celtics. Gordon Hayward's half-court heave that nearly won the 2010 title against Duke remains one of the most dramatic shots in tournament history. Historic Hinkle Fieldhouse, a National Historic Landmark, served as the inspiration for the movie 'Hoosiers.'
2086.mascotOrigin: The 'Bulldogs' nickname was adopted in the early 1900s; Butler's live bulldog mascot Blue has been a beloved game-day presence
2086.iconicMoment: Back-to-back NCAA Championship game appearances in 2010 and 2011, including Gordon Hayward's near-miss half-court shot against Duke in the 2010 final
2086.funFact: Hinkle Fieldhouse, Butler's home court since 1928, is a National Historic Landmark and served as the inspiration for the gymnasium in the movie 'Hoosiers'

239.blurb: Baylor's basketball program began in 1906 at the world's largest Baptist university, located in Waco, Texas. The 'Bears' mascot dates to 1914 when a live bear was brought to campus. Baylor's greatest moment came in 2021 when Scott Drew's Bears won the NCAA Championship, demolcing previously undefeated Gonzaga 86-70 in the title game. Drew had rebuilt the program from scratch after a tragedy-marred scandal in 2003, making the championship one of the most remarkable turnarounds in college sports history.
239.mascotOrigin: The first live bear mascot was brought to campus in 1914; the current live bears Joy and Lady are North American black bears that live on campus
239.iconicMoment: Winning the 2021 NCAA Championship by dominating undefeated Gonzaga 86-70, capping one of college basketball's greatest rebuilding stories under Scott Drew
239.funFact: When Scott Drew took over in 2003, the program was in disarray after a murder scandal; he rebuilt it from nearly nothing to a national championship in 18 years — one of the great turnaround stories in all of sports

2439.blurb: UNLV's basketball program was founded in 1958 and became a national power under Jerry Tarkanian, the legendary 'Tark the Shark.' The Runnin' Rebels won the 1990 NCAA Championship in dominant fashion, crushing Duke 103-73 — the largest margin of victory in title game history. Tarkanian was famous for chewing on a towel during games and for recruiting talented players others overlooked. The 1991 team went 34-0 before a stunning upset loss to Duke in the Final Four, denying what many considered one of the greatest teams ever assembled.
2439.mascotOrigin: The 'Rebels' nickname and Hey Reb mascot reflect the frontier spirit of Las Vegas and the American West
2439.iconicMoment: The 1990 NCAA Championship, a 103-73 demolition of Duke that set the record for largest margin of victory in a title game
2439.funFact: The 1991 UNLV team went 34-0 during the regular season and was heavily favored to repeat as champions, but was upset by Duke in the Final Four semifinal in one of the biggest upsets in tournament history

2350.blurb: Loyola Chicago, a Jesuit university founded in 1870, has played basketball since 1913. The 'Ramblers' nickname dates to the early days when the team had to travel across the city for games. Loyola won the 1963 NCAA Championship with a historic team that started four Black players — a first for a championship team during the Civil Rights era. The program captured hearts again in 2018 when 98-year-old team chaplain Sister Jean Dolores Schmidt became a national sensation during their Cinderella Final Four run.
2350.mascotOrigin: The 'Ramblers' name originated because the team had to 'ramble' across Chicago to find places to play in the program's early years
2350.iconicMoment: The 2018 Final Four run as an 11-seed, with 98-year-old team chaplain Sister Jean becoming a national phenomenon
2350.funFact: Loyola's 1963 championship team broke racial barriers by starting four Black players; the team's semifinal game against Mississippi State only happened because MSU players defied a state injunction prohibiting them from playing against integrated teams

2378.blurb: UMBC (University of Maryland, Baltimore County) has fielded a basketball program since 1967 and competes in the America East Conference. The 'Retrievers' mascot is a Chesapeake Bay Retriever, the state dog of Maryland. On March 16, 2018, UMBC made history by becoming the first No. 16 seed to defeat a No. 1 seed in the NCAA Tournament, stunning Virginia 74-54 in a result that shook college basketball to its core. The victory, led by Jairus Lyles' 28 points, remains the most significant upset in tournament history.
2378.mascotOrigin: Named the 'Retrievers' after the Chesapeake Bay Retriever, the official state dog of Maryland and a symbol of the Chesapeake Bay region
2378.iconicMoment: The historic 74-54 upset of No. 1 overall seed Virginia on March 16, 2018 — the first time a 16-seed ever defeated a 1-seed in the NCAA Tournament
2378.funFact: UMBC's stunning 20-point victory over Virginia was so unexpected that an estimated 97.5% of ESPN Tournament Challenge brackets had Virginia advancing — nearly all 18 million brackets were busted

87.blurb: Notre Dame's basketball program dates to 1897 and the 'Fighting Irish' nickname — whose exact origin is debated — became the school's official moniker in the 1920s. While Notre Dame is best known for football, the basketball program has a proud history including an upset of UCLA that ended the Bruins' record 88-game winning streak in 1974. Under Mike Brey, Notre Dame became a consistent ACC contender with multiple Sweet Sixteen appearances. The program plays at the Purcell Pavilion at the Edmund P. Joyce Center.
87.mascotOrigin: The exact origin of 'Fighting Irish' is debated — theories include references to the Irish Brigade in the Civil War and the school's Irish Catholic heritage
87.iconicMoment: Ending UCLA's NCAA-record 88-game winning streak with a 71-70 victory on January 19, 1974, one of college basketball's most historic upsets
87.funFact: Digger Phelps' Notre Dame team broke UCLA's legendary 88-game winning streak in 1974, and fans famously stormed the court and tore down the backboard

2483.blurb: Oregon's basketball program dates to 1903, and the Ducks won the first-ever NCAA Championship in 1939, defeating Ohio State 46-33 in Evanston, Illinois. The program's identity is deeply tied to Nike co-founder Phil Knight, an Oregon alumnus whose generous donations transformed the Ducks' facilities into among the finest in the nation. Matthew Knight Arena, opened in 2011, features state-of-the-art amenities. Oregon has returned to national prominence in recent years with multiple Sweet Sixteen and Final Four appearances.
2483.mascotOrigin: The 'Ducks' name is commonly associated with Oregon; the mascot is modeled after Disney's Donald Duck through a handshake agreement with Walt Disney himself in 1947
2483.iconicMoment: Winning the first-ever NCAA Championship in 1939, defeating Ohio State 46-33 to claim the inaugural title
2483.funFact: Oregon's mascot is based on Donald Duck thanks to a 1947 handshake agreement between then-athletic director Leo Harris and Walt Disney, making the Ducks the only school with a Disney-licensed mascot

120.blurb: Maryland's basketball program dates to 1904 and the 'Terrapins' nickname refers to the diamondback terrapin turtle native to the Chesapeake Bay region. The Terps won their only NCAA Championship in 2002 under Gary Williams, defeating Indiana behind tournament MOP Juan Dixon. Maryland was a founding member of the ACC before moving to the Big Ten in 2014. Legendary coach Lefty Driesell proclaimed he would make Maryland the 'UCLA of the East' and transformed the program into a national contender in the 1970s.
120.mascotOrigin: Named for the diamondback terrapin, a small turtle native to Maryland's Chesapeake Bay region and the official state reptile
120.iconicMoment: Winning the 2002 NCAA Championship under Gary Williams, with Juan Dixon leading the Terps past Indiana for the school's only national title
120.funFact: Lefty Driesell famously declared he would make Maryland the 'UCLA of the East' when he was hired in 1969, and while he didn't win a title, he built the program into a perennial national power

2550.blurb: Seton Hall's basketball program dates to 1903, making it one of the oldest Catholic university basketball programs in the nation. The 'Pirates' nickname was chosen in the 1930s to reflect the school's New Jersey coastal heritage. Seton Hall reached the 1989 NCAA Championship game under P.J. Carlesimo, falling to Michigan in overtime on Rumeal Robinson's free throws — one of the most dramatic title games ever played. As a founding member of the Big East, the Pirates have been a consistent presence in one of basketball's premier conferences.
2550.mascotOrigin: The 'Pirates' nickname was adopted in the 1930s, reflecting New Jersey's coastal heritage and seafaring history
2550.iconicMoment: The 1989 NCAA Championship game against Michigan, a classic that went to overtime with the title decided on free throws in the final seconds
2550.funFact: Seton Hall was the first Big East team from New Jersey to reach the NCAA Championship game, and the 1989 title game remains one of the most dramatic finals in tournament history

2507.blurb: Providence College, a Dominican Catholic institution founded in 1917, began its basketball program in 1921. The 'Friars' nickname naturally reflects the school's Dominican religious heritage. Providence has been a Big East Conference member since the league's founding in 1979 and reached the Final Four in 1973 and 1987. The program has produced notable NBA players including Jimmy Walker and Billy Donovan, who went on to become one of the most successful coaches in college basketball history at Florida.
2507.mascotOrigin: The 'Friars' name reflects Providence College's founding by Dominican friars, members of the Order of Preachers
2507.iconicMoment: The 1987 Final Four run under Rick Pitino, marking Providence's second and most recent trip to the national semifinals
2507.funFact: Former Providence player Billy Donovan went on to become the head coach at Florida, winning back-to-back national championships in 2006 and 2007

2168.blurb: Dayton's basketball program dates to 1903 at the Catholic university founded by the Society of Mary in 1850. The 'Flyers' nickname honors the Wright Brothers, who conducted their aviation experiments in Dayton, Ohio. The program won the 1962 NIT Championship and reached the NCAA Championship game in 1967. Dayton is also known as the permanent host of the NCAA Tournament's First Four games at UD Arena, and the passionate Flyer faithful have helped create one of the most electric atmospheres in college basketball.
2168.mascotOrigin: The 'Flyers' nickname honors Wilbur and Orville Wright, the aviation pioneers who conducted their flying experiments in Dayton, Ohio
2168.iconicMoment: Reaching the 1967 NCAA Championship game, the highest point in Flyers basketball history
2168.funFact: Dayton has been the permanent host of the NCAA Tournament's First Four games since 2001, making UD Arena one of the most important venues in March Madness history

305.blurb: DePaul's basketball program was established in 1923 at the Chicago Catholic university founded in 1898 by the Vincentian Fathers. The 'Blue Demons' name combines the school's blue colors with a spirited moniker. Under legendary coach Ray Meyer, who coached for 42 seasons, DePaul became a national power, reaching the Final Four in 1943 and 1979. Meyer's son Joey continued the coaching dynasty. DePaul produced NBA great George Mikan, widely considered the first dominant big man in basketball history, and Mark Aguirre.
305.mascotOrigin: The 'Blue Demons' name combines the school's signature blue color with an energetic and fierce spirit; the mascot is named DIBS (DePaul Intercollegiate Blue Spirit)
305.iconicMoment: George Mikan's dominant career in the 1940s, revolutionizing the center position and leading DePaul to the 1945 NIT Championship
305.funFact: George Mikan was so dominant at DePaul that the NCAA introduced the goaltending rule largely because of him — he went on to become the NBA's first superstar with the Minneapolis Lakers

235.blurb: Memphis basketball began in 1920 and the program became a national powerhouse under several notable coaches. The 'Tigers' nickname was adopted in the 1910s. Larry Finch, a Memphis native and legend, played on the team that reached the 1973 NCAA Championship game and later coached the program. Under John Calipari, Memphis reached the 2008 title game before Mario Chalmers' three-pointer forced overtime in favor of Kansas. The program has been one of the most successful non-power-conference teams in college basketball history.
235.mascotOrigin: The 'Tigers' nickname was adopted in the early 1900s, with the live mascot TOM (Tigers of Memphis) appearing at games
235.iconicMoment: The 2008 NCAA Championship game against Kansas, where Memphis led by nine points with two minutes left before Mario Chalmers' miraculous three-pointer forced overtime
235.funFact: Memphis has produced more NBA players than almost any non-power-conference school, including Penny Hardaway (who later returned as head coach), Derrick Rose, and Tyreke Evans

2.blurb: Auburn's basketball program dates to 1906, and the 'Tigers' nickname has been associated with Auburn athletics since the university's earliest days — inspired by a line from Oliver Goldsmith's poem 'The Deserted Village' referencing a 'bold peasantry.' While historically a football school, Auburn's basketball program has experienced a dramatic rise under Bruce Pearl, who brought energy, sellout crowds, and a Final Four appearance in 2019. Charles Barkley, one of the NBA's most iconic personalities, starred at Auburn from 1981-1984 before being drafted fifth overall.
2.mascotOrigin: The 'Tigers' nickname has been used since the 1890s, inspired partly by a line from Oliver Goldsmith's poem 'The Deserted Village'; the live eagle mascot War Eagle is a separate tradition from the Tigers name
2.iconicMoment: Reaching the 2019 Final Four under Bruce Pearl, the program's first Final Four appearance in over 30 years, energizing the fan base
2.funFact: Charles Barkley was listed as 6-foot-6 at Auburn but later admitted he was closer to 6-foot-4 — he went on to become one of basketball's greatest and most entertaining personalities
